bounded_excerpt: 2 Last revision: March 25, 2026 Guideline Resource Unit Series Note This document is a multi-chapter guidel ine series on radiation dermatitis. Chapter 1 addresses risk reduction strategies initiated befor e or during radiation therapy (RT) to reduce the incidence and/or severity of acute radiation dermatitis (ARD). Chapter 2 focusses on management of ARD and recall reactions. Chapter 3 provides an overview of interventions for chronic radiation dermatitis (CRD), including long-term skin effects. Background Radiation dermatitis is a side effect of RT that results from direct radiation-induced injury and the subsequent inflammatory response. Skin reactions may appear at both the entry and exit points of the radiation beam. Available evidence suggests that the risk and seve rity of radiation dermatitis are influenced by patient-, disease-, and treatment-rela ted factors, although there is variability across studies in the factors reported and the st rength of their associations. (2025) , Canadian experts specializing in breast cancer and ARD.10 1. Gentle washing of the tr eated skin with lukewarm water and a mild, unscented, pH-balanced cleanser is recommended daily, with bathing limit ed to under 10 minutes and the skin gently pat- dried with a soft towel, avoiding any friction.10 2. Moisturizers should be applied at least twice da ily, preferably after washi ng, using products that are water-based, unscented, non- comedogenic, and hypoallergenic.10 3. Sun exposure to the treated skin should be avoided by using protec tive clothing, hats, shade, and UVA/UVB-protective sunscreen with SPF ≥ 30.10 4. Swimming is allowed unless skin breakdown or moist desquamation is present; treated skin should be rinsed and moisturized immediately afterw ard to remove residual chlorine. Hot tubs, steam rooms, and saunas should be avoided due to excessive heat.10 5. Shaving in the treatm ent area is acceptable unless the skin is sensitive, irritated, affected by dermatitis; an electric shaver is preferred, an

bounded_excerpt: 5 Last revision: March 25, 2026 Guideline Resource Unit Barrier Films and Barrier-Forming Creams 8. Barrier film with silicone adhesion (Mepitel® Film) may be recommended for patients receiving breast or chest wall RT, reducing severity and improving recovery (Level of Evidence: I17-19 and II20-22; Strength of Recommendation: A) ; and is optional for patients receiving head or neck RT, with limited efficacy, low adherence to stubble, and often not well tolerated (Level of Evidence: II23, 24; Strength of Recommendation: C) . For patients receiving RT to other sites, use should be considered on a case-by-case basis due to the absence of direct evidence and limited research. 9. Silicone-based barrier-forming gel (StrataXRT®) generally may be recommended for patients receiving breast RT, lowering severity (Level of Evidence: II25; Strength of Recommendation: B) ; and generally recommended for head and neck RT, re ducing incidence of grade 2 and 3 ARD (Level of Evidence: I26; Strength of Recommendation: B) . Note that a meta-analysis and two RCTs indicated reduced performance or inferiority in breast cancer patient s compared to Mepitel Film.21, 22, 27 10. Polymer-based barrier-forming

bounded_excerpt: 9 Last revision: March 25, 2026 Guideline Resource Unit Grade 2 and 3 Acute Radiation Dermatitis Progression to Grade 2 and 3 ARD involves tender or bright erythema, mo ist desquamation (ranging from patchy and confined to skin folds and creases, to confluent desquamati on outside of skin folds and creases), and edema (fro m moderate to pitting). Patients may report pain, burning, itching, pulling, and tenderness. The goal of treatment is to maintain ski n integrity and hydration where possible, reduce discomfort and pain, and to reduce risk of complications of further trauma and infection. 7. Encourage patients to c ontinue cleaning the affected area, even when ulcerated. 8. To alleviate irritated skin, advise patients to a pply a cool (saline) compress or soak the affected skin in room temperature saline. If treating foot, hand, or anogenita l area, prepare as a sitz bath. Follow with a moisture-retentive barrier (e.g., dressing coated with Glaxal Base, CeraVe or Vaseline) in areas without moist desquamation. 9. bounded_excerpt: 11 Last revision: March 25, 2026 Guideline Resource Unit Chapter 3. Chronic Radiation Dermatitis Background Chronic radiation dermatitis (CRD), also known as la te-radiation tissue injury, constitutes a spectrum of progressive, irreversible changes in the skin and underlying soft tissue t hat develop more than 90 days after the completion of RT. These changes are the result of latent cellular reactions to radiation exposure, leading to alterations in the skin such as vascularity, pigmentation, and fibrotic tissue changes, which can occur in various gradations (see Appendix B ). These skin reactions can become apparent many months or even years after the course of RT has concluded. Table 1 highlights key differences between ARD and CRD. Risk factors for CRD overlap with t hose for ARD, and include anatomical location, radiation dose, fractionation, radiation volume, and bolus use. Additi onal risk factors include re irradiation, underlying connective tissue disorders, and a history of smoking. Given that CRD is often irreversible and can signifi cantly impair a patient's quality of life, early recognition is important. Although ther e are no gold standards for CRD treatment,71 treat

bounded_excerpt: 12 Last revision: March 25, 2026 Guideline Resource Unit Chronic Ulceration and Necrosis Non-healing wounds in the irradiat ed treatment field are a result of impaired vascularization and tissue hypoxia. Nonhealing ulcers could also be a second malignancy and should be managed as such. 1. Surgical debridement of necrotic tissue may be recommended as first line treatment in patients with adequate arterial supply, foll owed by surgical reconstruction. Exercise caution with lower extremity ulcers which may fail to heal due to underlying arterial or venous insufficiency. 2. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy may be consider ed as an adjuvant treatment to promote wound healing (Level of Evidence: II74, V75, 76 [head and neck, pelvic canc er patients]; Strength of Recommendation: C) . However, it is contraindicat ed for patients currently receiving chemotherapy.70 Telangiectasia Telangiectasia is characterized by damaged, visibl e blood vessels just below the skin or mucous membranes. For head and neck cancer patients, lipotransfer may be considered to reduce fibrosis and restore volume loss (Level of Evidence V85; Strength of Re commendation C). 8. For breast cancer patients who have undergone a lumpectomy and who continue to experience severe fibrosis after conventional treatments, partial mastectomy and reconstructions may be considered (Level of Evidence IV86, Strength of Reco mmendation C). Skin Colour Change Colour change in irradiated skin could consist of hy perpigmentation (characterized by local dilatation of small vessels resulting in red discolora tion of the skin or mucous membranes) and hypopigmentation (characterized by loss of skin pigment, e.g., vitiligo). Patient-reported hyperpigmentation can affect up to 80% of breast cancer survivors. 87 9. Laser therapy, especially Q-switched laser, may be considered to manage hyperpigmentation (Level of Evidence V80, Strength of Recommendation C).

bounded_excerpt: 24 Last revision: March 25, 2026 Guideline Resource Unit Development and Revision History This guideline was developed by members from the Alberta Provincial Breast Tumour Team, Alberta Provincial Head and Neck Tumour Team, and a methodologist from the Guideline Resource Unit. The draft guideline was externally reviewed and endorsed by Alberta radiation oncologists and members of the clinical education team who were not involved in the guideline’s development, including radiation t herapists. A detailed description of the methodology followed during the guideline development process can be found in the Guideline Resource Unit Handbook.

bounded_excerpt: This guideline was originally developed in 2025 (focus on prevention only) and updated in March 2026 (added management chapters). Levels of Evidence I Evidence from at least one large randomized, controlled trial of good methodological qua lity (low potential for bias) or meta-analyses of well-conducted randomized trials without heterogeneity II Small randomized trials or large randomized trials with a suspicion of bias (lower met hodological quality) or meta- analyses of such trials or of trials with demonstrated heterogeneity III Prospective cohort studies IV Retrospective cohort studies or case-control studies V Studies without control group, case reports, expert opinion Strength of Recommendations A Strong evidence for efficacy with a substantial clinical benefit; strongly recommended B Strong or moderate evidence for efficacy but with a limited clinical benefit; generally recommended C Insufficient evidence for efficacy or benefit does not outweigh the risk or the dis advantages (adverse events, costs, etc.); optional D Moderate evidence against efficacy or for adverse outcome; generally not recommended E Strong evidence against efficacy or for adverse outcome; never recommend
